lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent cane mills or refineries repeatedly providing toll milling, toll refining, or comparable outsourced conversion services to external cane growers, raw-sugar owners, or food customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Vertically integrated farm-to-sugar operations, grower-owned cooperative processing of member cane, commodity or branded sugar sales without an outsourced-service relationship, captive facilities, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring conversion fees or processing margins for transforming customer-directed cane or raw sugar into raw or refined sugar; the eligible operator is paid for accountable manufacturing rather than merely selling its own sugar inventory.
lens.deviation_from_default: The 2022 NAICS code combines sugarcane processing and raw-cane-sugar refining, and observed firms include vertically integrated, cooperative, and stand-alone stages. The lens is narrowed to independent repeat toll or contract conversion so the screened activity is a coherent outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader sugar and confectionery industry rather than cane sugar alone: production is 42.74% of employment, transportation/material moving 15.26%, maintenance 5.54%, office support 5.73%, and management 5.40%. I map planning, documentation, quality analysis, forecasting, dispatch, process optimization, and some control-room work to AI exposure, while treating most milling, refining, material handling, sanitation, repair, and field work as physical. Confectionery's retail and hand-production mix makes the bias direction uncertain; modern integrated cane facilities are already highly automated, which likely makes a generic mapping optimistic.
a.rationale: Cane mills and refineries are capital-intensive process plants. AI can improve predictive maintenance, energy and recovery optimization, crop and raw-sugar scheduling, quality analysis, customer service, and back-office tasks, but most remaining line and maintenance labor is embodied and safety critical.
a.change_evidence: Wage-weighted occupation and task-time data from Louisiana mills, Florida integrated mills/refineries, and stand-alone refineries, combined with an inventory of existing automation and contractor use, would materially change a.
caveats: No eligible LMM operator exists in the frozen dataset estimate, so this is an overall-industry proxy.
The BLS occupation source includes confectionery and is not cane-specific.
Installed DCS, laboratory, logistics, and packaging automation is not observed plant by plant.

rho.bridge: A British sugar producer's AI and predictive-maintenance program and US manufacturing-wide adoption surveys demonstrate technical feasibility and diffusion, not realized five-year implementation in US LMM cane-sugar service firms. The reference plants and survey respondents are generally larger and better resourced, biasing rho upward relative to an LMM operator; short harvest campaigns, legacy OT, food-safety validation, cybersecurity, and uptime risk further reduce transferability.
rho.rationale: Administrative copilots and analytics can deploy quickly, while predictive maintenance and process optimization benefit from the rich sensor data of continuous plants. Full labor substitution requires reliable integration with critical controls and must survive high-throughput harvest operations, making implementation partial.
rho.change_evidence: Audited US cane-mill and refinery deployments showing labor hours avoided, uptime, recovery, energy use, false alarms, model drift, cybersecurity review, and time from pilot to stable production would materially move rho.
caveats: The cane-specific AI case is outside the United States.
Manufacturing survey definitions may count pilots or lightweight office tools.
The absence of a current LMM lens firm makes capital and staffing assumptions hypothetical.

e.rationale: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the LMM EBITDA band, so there is no nonzero denominator on which to estimate the eligible share. Public evidence shows vertically integrated and cooperative structures and a historical toll arrangement, but it does not identify a current LMM outsourced-service firm.
e.change_evidence: A verified legal-entity census of US cane mills and refineries with EBITDA, ownership, customer contract type, and current toll-processing revenue could establish whether the frozen zero count hides an eligible entity.
caveats: Missing is not equivalent to zero eligibility; the denominator is estimated as zero.
The frozen n input is itself an ESTIMATE based on margin-bridged size classes.
NAICS entity and establishment boundaries may differ for integrated corporate groups.

s5.rationale: No eligible LMM firm was identified and the frozen eligible population denominator is zero, so a five-year qualifying control-transfer probability cannot be estimated without inventing a target population.
s5.change_evidence: If eligible firms are found, PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, cooperative records, and state property and corporate filings covering US cane-mill and refinery control transfers over 15 to 20 years would support an estimate.
caveats: Sales of integrated corporate groups, member-interest changes, internal reorganizations, and plant closures are not automatically qualifying transfers.
Historical toll-refining evidence does not establish current independent ownership.
A tiny population would make any observed rate unstable.

q.rationale: No current LMM toll-milling or toll-refining agreement was located. A decades-old customs ruling confirms that toll refining has existed, but it provides no current fee, repricing, productivity-sharing, or customer-switching evidence from which to estimate five-year retained benefit.
q.change_evidence: Current tolling MSAs and bid sheets showing conversion fees, raw-material ownership, energy indexation, term, throughput guarantees, productivity clauses, renewal cadence, and termination rights are required to estimate q.
caveats: Commodity sugar prices and federal policy do not reveal how an outsourced processor shares AI savings.
Vertically integrated and cooperative economics are not substitutes for external-customer contracts.
The frozen LMM count of zero leaves no observed operator whose commercial terms can anchor retention.

d5.bridge: USDA's May 2026 outlook forecasts total US sugar use unchanged at 12.389 million STRV between 2025/26 and 2026/27 and cane production declining from 4.218 million to 4.088 million STRV. Its 2025 long-term baseline projected total deliveries rising with population. These figures measure commodity production and demand, not outsourced LMM cane-processing services. Imports, beet substitution, refinery sourcing, and the disappearance of any eligible firm can cause lens demand to diverge in either direction.
d5.rationale: Sugar remains a recurring input for food and beverage manufacturers, supporting stable physical throughput. Domestic cane volume is exposed to weather, water, acreage, health policy, imports, and mill closures, while new refining capacity and customer shifts can redistribute rather than eliminate demand.
d5.change_evidence: USDA processor-level five-year cane production and allotment forecasts, refinery raw-sugar sourcing, announced capacity changes, and current toll customers' sourcing plans would materially tighten d5.
caveats: The current-service-basket demand is unobserved because no eligible LMM provider is identified.
USDA use data combine beet, cane, and imported sugar.
Production forecasts can differ from demand through inventories and trade adjustments.

o.bridge: Company and industry sources show physical mills and refineries processing large cane volumes, and FDA requires covered food facilities to maintain accountable hazard controls and food-safety plans. This establishes that software alone cannot supply refined sugar, but not that a current LMM outsourced operator will supply it. The proxy overstates lens persistence if volume consolidates into integrated or cooperative facilities outside the lens.
o.rationale: AI can reduce labor and improve controls, but year-five sugar quantity still requires accountable physical milling, clarification, evaporation, crystallization, refining, packaging, and shipment. Only a small amount could be eliminated through software; the main substitution is among operator types.
o.change_evidence: Five-year make-versus-buy plans from growers and raw-sugar owners, autonomous-plant designs, facility closure and capacity announcements, and toll-versus-integrated throughput shares would move o.
caveats: Operator requirement does not imply survival of a qualifying LMM outsourced provider.
Cane, beet, and imported refined sugar can substitute at the customer level.
Automation may reduce staffing sharply while leaving the facility operator necessary.

narrative.executive_view: Cane sugar offers credible plant-optimization use cases for AI, but the frozen dataset estimates no firms in the target EBITDA band. Public industry evidence is dominated by vertically integrated and cooperative operations, leaving no defensible current LMM outsourced-service population.
narrative.ai_impact: Predictive maintenance, energy and recovery optimization, raw-material scheduling, quality analytics, production reporting, and back-office copilots can improve performance. Most remaining work is physical, safety critical, and embedded in already automated process plants.
narrative.value_capture: Value capture cannot be estimated for the lens without current toll contracts. Commodity prices, federal allotments, and integrated economics do not reveal whether an external customer would reclaim conversion savings at renewal.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen n input is zero. Large integrated Florida operations, Louisiana cooperatives and mills, and major refineries demonstrate a functioning industry, but not an acquirable LMM recurring-service firm; historical tolling does not cure the current evidence gap.
narrative.demand_durability: US sugar use is broadly stable and physical cane processing remains necessary, although domestic cane output varies with crops, weather, water, closures, imports, and beet competition. Operator-required demand is durable in the industry, not necessarily in the absent target lens.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal uncertainty is whether an eligible firm exists at all. Additional risks are legacy OT integration, cyber and food safety, campaign downtime, environmental compliance, commodity and energy exposure, federal policy dependence, and consolidation into much larger operators.
narrative.principal_driver: High-throughput sensor-rich mills and refineries can benefit from AI-enabled maintenance, optimization, quality, and planning.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen LMM firm count is zero, preventing defensible estimates of eligibility, transfer probability, or commercial retention.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: Cane Sugar Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines 311314 as establishments processing sugarcane and/or refining cane sugar from raw cane sugar, confirming that the code combines milling and refining stages.

sources.title: Florida Crystals Sugar Refining
sources.supports: Describes a fully vertically integrated cane sugar company that harvests, mills, refines, packages, and delivers sugar and runs its Florida refinery year-round.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Sugar and Confectionery Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports broader-industry employment and wages, including 42.74% production, 15.26% transportation/material moving, 5.54% maintenance, 5.73% office support, and 5.40% management.

sources.title: British Sugar builds an AI-powered data organization
sources.supports: Documents AI, IoT sensors, machine learning, and data analytics used to create predictive-maintenance capabilities at a sugar producer.

sources.title: U.S. Sugar: Our Purpose and Operations
sources.supports: Describes a fully integrated, advanced sugarcane processing facility and states that the Clewiston mill and refinery can process up to 42,000 tons of cane per day.

sources.title: NAM to White House: Here's How to Boost AI Adoption in Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports a 2025 survey in which 51% of manufacturers used AI, 60% expected to by 2027, and 80% said AI would be vital by 2030.

sources.title: USDA Sugar and Sweeteners Outlook: May 2026
sources.supports: Forecasts US total sugar use at 12.389 million STRV in both 2025/26 and 2026/27 and cane sugar production declining from 4.218 million to 4.088 million STRV.

sources.title: American Sugar Producers File Section 301 Comments
sources.supports: Reports that 12% of US cane sugar mills and refineries closed over the prior decade, including the loss of Texas cane farming in 2024.

sources.title: Louisiana Raw Sugar Factories
sources.supports: Reports 11 operating Louisiana raw-sugar factories and attributes fewer mills alongside high production to increasing factory efficiency.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Requires covered food facilities to maintain hazard analysis, preventive controls, food-safety plans, and current good manufacturing practices.

sources.title: Customs Ruling HQ 224418 on toll refining
sources.supports: Provides historical evidence of a US arrangement under which raw sugar was tolled into refined sugar, but does not establish current LMM eligibility or contract economics.
